text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic Whipple procedure, starting with the patient in Trendelenburg position. The medial approach is used to fully mobilize the duodenum, exposing its posterior surface. After mobilization, the groove of the superior mesenteric vein is identified. The bowel distal to the ligament of Treitz is divided using a stapler and delivered into the right upper quadrant.

With the patient in reverse Trendelenburg position, the surgeon completes dropping the hepatic flexure and opens the lesser sac to expose the anterior surface of the pancreas. The superior mesenteric vein is skeletonized, and the gastrocolic trunk is isolated and divided. A tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck is visible, showing the confluence of veins. A Nathanson liver retractor is used to keep the liver out of the way as the dissection moves superior to the pancreas. The stomach is divided just proximal to the pylorus, and the pancreas is clearly visible. The hepatic artery and gastroduodenal artery are skeletonized and identified, with the gastroduodenal artery subsequently tied off and divided.

The surgeon confirms the development of the tunnel posterior to the pancreatic neck using a back scratcher. The Thunder Beat device is used to divide the pancreatic neck, with the SMV portal vein confluence visible. The bile duct is identified and isolated, with a lateral view showing a lymph node being dropped down. The right hepatic artery is visible and preserved. A bulldog clamp controls the bile duct to prevent bile spillage, and the duct is divided. The uncinate process is divided and separated using surgical staplers, leaving a small cuff of tissue near the SMA intact.

The surgeon places the resected specimen into a specimen retrieval bag for extraction through a fan and steel incision for frozen section analysis. A cholecystectomy is performed, and the gallbladder is placed in a separate specimen retrieval bag. Despite the sharp division of the pancreas at the duct level, the duct is not clearly visible. The surgical field appears clean and dry as the surgeon prepares for reconstruction.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas from the branches of the celiac and the splenic vein posteriorly, deploying stay sutures along the marginal vessels on the inferior and superior edges of the pancreas. A posterior gastrotomy is performed using the Thunderbeat, followed by placing a purse string suture around the gastrotomy site. A Maryland instrument is introduced to pull stay sutures, allowing the pancreas remnant to invaginate into the stomach for a pancreatic gastrostomy. The purse string suture is tied down tightly, and the anterior gastrotomy is closed.

From a lateral view of the right flank, the surgeon carefully orients the bowel for the hepatico-jejunostomy, which is performed using a single layer hand-sewn technique with a monofilament 5-0 suture. The ductomucosa anastomosis is completed, showing no biliary leak when checked with a clean white sponge. An anti-colic gastrojejunostomy is set up, and the common channel is created using a stapler. The gastroenterostomy is closed in two layers with running braided 3-0 sutures, completing the final stitch. The anastomosis appears well-constructed.
